ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific midloin chop code; A00LC (lamb meat, raw) is the closest defensible parent code. Lean-only preparation noted in record.

serving.description: 1 raw lean lamb midloin chop (bone-in, fat trimmed)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw lean lamb midloin chop with bone weighs approximately 120 g, yielding around 85–90 g of edible lean meat after cooking losses.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, loin chop, raw, lean — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for lamb midloin chop, raw, lean (cr_id F06050017). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 17174 (Lamb, loin, separable lean only, raw) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat set to -1 as not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb midloin chop, raw, lean contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content; numeric value set to 0 as a sentinel indicating no glycaemic response. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI testing is performed on pure protein/fat foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (71 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (330 mg K : 71 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Lean lamb midloin chop is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium marinades, as its naturally low sodium content and moderate potassium level support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (28.6 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With no carbohydrate or sugar content, lean lamb midloin chop has no glycaemic impact and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a high-quality protein source; avoid sugar-containing glazes or marinades that would add carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (73 mg/100 g) and low total fat (5 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.9 g/100 g; red meat classification warrants moderation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean lamb midloin chop is lower in saturated fat than many red meat cuts and may be consumed in moderate portions (up to 2–3 times per week) as part of a heart-healthy diet, provided it is grilled or baked without added saturated fats and accompanied by vegetables and whole grains.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) which can amplify inflammatory mediators; no significant anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit lean lamb midloin chop to occasional consumption, pairing it with anti-inflammatory accompaniments such as turmeric, ginger, or omega-3-rich foods, and preferring fatty fish or plant-based proteins as primary protein sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb midloin chop, raw, lean is a bone-in cut taken from the loin section of a lamb carcass, with all separable fat removed, leaving only the lean muscle meat. The raw meat displays a deep red colour with a tender, fine-grained texture and minimal visible marbling after fat trimming. It is a nutrient-dense, high-protein food providing 28.6 g of protein per 100 g and only 160 kcal, making it one of the leaner red meat options available. Total fat is 5.0 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.9 g, substantially lower than untrimmed lamb cuts. Carbohydrate content is zero, making this food suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 71 mg per 100 g, and potassium is 330 mg per 100 g, supporting a favourable electrolyte profile for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is 73 mg per 100 g, consistent with lean red meat. Iron content is 1.9 mg per 100 g, contributing to dietary iron intake, particularly haem iron which is highly bioavailable. This cut is commonly used in Australian, New Zealand, Middle Eastern, and South Asian cuisines, and is halal-certified when sourced from approved abattoirs. It is typically grilled, pan-fried, or barbecued. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension when prepared without added salt or sugar, and should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its red meat classification.

notes: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb midloin chop (lean muscle meat) contains no carbohydrate by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle food with no plant-derived fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and micronutrient data beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this specific entry (F06050017); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 17174 was consulted for saturated fat estimation only. glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value indicating no glycaemic response; gi_category is not_applicable because this food contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g and GI testing is not performed on pure protein/fat foods; GL per serving is 0.0. This food is classified as a generic raw ingredient, not a Singaporean dish; therefore multilingual local-name requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply under G6 of the scoring model. Cuisine is classified as Generic / Western. Halal suitability applies when sourced from an approved halal-certified abattoir.
